title: Iran ceasefire declared 'active' — oil above $100, CPI at 3.8%, ink running out
narrative: The US administration has called its month-long ceasefire with Iran 'active,' positioning Tehran as the recalcitrant party blocking peace. The physical economy tells a different story. Brent crude has structurally settled above $100 per barrel — not a spike, but a floor. US CPI spiked to 3.8% in the latest reading, the highest since May 2023. Snack giants have switched to black-and-white packaging because the ink isn't getting through. Shipping rerouted around the Cape of Good Hope months ago and hasn't come back. If the ceasefire were holding, these symptoms would be normalizing. Instead they are compounding. Either the blockade is outside ceasefire terms — making 'active' a fiction that covers ongoing interdiction — or the political theater is deliberately obscuring that kinetic restrictions never stopped.
telemetry: Brent crude settled above $100/bbl — structural elevated price, not a temporary spike
US CPI hit 3.8% — highest since May 2023 — driven in part by energy input costs that a genuine ceasefire should be relieving
Major snack corporations switched to black-and-white packaging — ink shortages from supply chain disruption
Cape of Good Hope shipping rerouting established as permanent for most carriers, adding 14 days and significant cost per voyage
US Treasury issued 'at sea' waivers for Iranian oil — effectively bypassing SWIFT-cutoff mechanism
Naval blockade remains in effect — ceasefire 'active' while interdiction operations continue
analysis:
The ceasefire terms gap is the story. If the blockade is outside ceasefire scope, then 'active' is technically accurate but functionally misleading — the pressure that drove Iran to the table remains in place, and the symptom set (oil, inflation, supply gaps) is the direct output of that pressure. If the blockade is supposed to have lifted and hasn't, then 'active' is a lie. Either way, the administration is getting the political win of a ceasefire while maintaining the economic pressure that makes the ceasefire meaningful to Tehran. The public narrative covers the policy outcome.

The snack-packaging switch is the most viscerally legible data point. A major corporation does not absorb the cost of reprinting packaging for a temporary disruption. The ink is not getting through because the supply chain feeding it has been disrupted long enough that deprinting is the rational business response. That is not a ceasefire signal. That is a structural supply break that a ceasefire should have reopened.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The ceasefire is a press release. The blockade is the policy. The symptoms — $100 oil, 3.8% inflation, empty ink aisles — are not glitches in a ceasefire that is working. They are evidence of what the ceasefire was designed to leave intact.
